does: This paper develops a theoretical framework for understanding machine unlearning by analyzing stochastic gradient descent through the lens of verification error—the L2 distance between weights of approximately unlearned models and naively retrained models. The authors taxonomize existing unlearning approaches and metrics, then argue that verification error subsumes a large class of other unlearning metrics and should be the primary optimization target. Through theoretical analysis of SGD, they identify key variables influencing verification error and derive an easy-to-compute proxy metric that practitioners can use to predict unlearning performance without full retraining. The work provides both theoretical insights into which factors matter for effective unlearning and practical tools for evaluating approximate unlearning methods.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on machine unlearning, which emerged from privacy and data deletion requirements. It extends prior research that proposed various approximate unlearning algorithms as alternatives to prohibitively expensive full retraining, while addressing the fragmented landscape of evaluation metrics. The paper connects to foundational optimization theory, particularly the analysis of SGD dynamics, adapting classical optimization insights to the unlearning setting. It also relates to broader work on understanding neural network training trajectories and weight-space analysis, leveraging these perspectives to formalize what makes an unlearning procedure effective. The emphasis on verification error as a unifying metric responds to limitations in existing ad-hoc evaluation approaches scattered across prior unlearning literature.
enables: This theoretical framework enables more principled design of unlearning algorithms by identifying which training factors most strongly influence unlearning quality. The easy-to-compute proxy for verification error could accelerate development and evaluation of new unlearning methods by reducing computational requirements for assessing candidate approaches. The work points toward data-efficient unlearning strategies by clarifying which aspects of SGD trajectories matter most, potentially informing hyperparameter choices and algorithmic modifications that improve unlearning with minimal additional cost. These insights could support practical deployment of machine unlearning in regulated settings requiring data deletion, while the theoretical foundations may extend to other approximate inference problems where discrepancy from full retraining is difficult to measure directly.
